title: Quantization and Vector Quantization for Gaussian Attributes

learningObjectives.description: Apply scalar quantization to 3DGS attributes, selecting appropriate bit depths for position (14–24 bit), SH AC coefficients (5–8 bit), and opacity (8 bit) based on attribute sensitivity analysis
Explain how entropy coding exploits non-uniform distributions in quantized Gaussian attributes for additional compression beyond scalar quantization alone
Distinguish scalar quantization from vector quantization and identify when VQ's exploitation of inter-attribute correlations justifies its codebook memory and training overhead
Explain RDO-VQ's entropy-penalized training objective and state how minimizing the code length of VQ indices differs from minimizing reconstruction error alone

content:
## Source Coding Basics

3DGS attributes are stored as float32 (32-bit IEEE 754). This precision is almost certainly excessive for most attributes. The core question: **how many bits does each attribute actually need?**

## Scalar Quantization

Scalar quantization maps a continuous value \(x\) to a discrete code:

\[\hat{x} = \Delta \cdot \text{round}(x / \Delta)\]

where \(\Delta\) is the quantization step size. With \(B\) bits, you can represent \(2^B\) distinct values.

**Per-attribute analysis** for the Truck scene (2.54M Gaussians):

| Attribute | Float32 bits | Bits needed | Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position xyz | 32 | 24 (with contraction: 14) | 1.3–2.3× |
| SH DC color | 32 | 8 | 4× |
| Scale | 32 | 6 | 5.3× |
| Opacity | 32 | 6 | 5.3× |
| Rotation | 32 | 6 | 5.3× |
| SH AC coeffs | 32 | 5 | 6.4× |

Weighted average across all 59 attributes: approximately **5.94× size reduction** from quantization alone (SOG paper). Starting from ~630 MB for the Truck scene, quantization brings it to ~106 MB.

### Non-Linear Positional Quantization

Gaussians in unbounded scenes (Mip-NeRF 360 style) are spatially contracted: most Gaussians cluster near the scene center, with very few in the far background. Uniform quantization wastes most of its precision on the empty far field.

SOG and MipNeRF360 apply a non-linear spatial contraction \(f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to [-1,1]^3\) before quantization, giving denser quantization levels near the scene center where most Gaussians actually are.

## Entropy Coding

Quantized values are rarely uniformly distributed. Most attributes cluster around specific values (e.g., opacity near 1.0 for visible surfaces, near 0 for transparent regions). **Entropy coding** exploits this:

- **Huffman coding**: assign shorter codes to more frequent symbols. Theoretically optimal for symbol-by-symbol coding.
- **Arithmetic coding**: encode the full sequence at once; achieves the entropy lower bound \(H = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i\) bits/symbol.
- **Prediction + residual coding**: predict the next value from context (e.g., neighboring Gaussians in a sorted grid), then entropy-code only the small residual.

Typical entropy coding gains for 3DGS attributes: **1.15–1.4× additional reduction** on top of quantization.

## Vector Quantization (VQ)

Scalar quantization treats each attribute independently. **VQ** exploits correlations between attributes of the same Gaussian by clustering full attribute vectors:

1. **Learn a codebook** \(\{e_k\}_{k=1}^K\) of \(K\) representative vectors.
2. **Encode** each Gaussian by its nearest codebook index: \(\text{idx}_i = \arg\min_k \|a_i - e_k\|\).
3. **Decode** by table lookup: \(\hat{a}_i = e_{\text{idx}_i}\).

Storage: instead of 59 × 4 = 236 bytes per Gaussian, you store \(\lceil \log_2 K \rceil\) bits per Gaussian plus the codebook of size \(K \times 59 \times 4\) bytes.

### Sensitivity-Aware VQ (Compressed3D)

Not all attributes contribute equally to rendered image quality. Opacity errors are perceptually more damaging than SH AC coefficient errors. Compressed3D weights the quantization error by per-attribute **sensitivity** (the gradient of rendered image quality w.r.t. each attribute), allocating more codebook precision to high-sensitivity attributes.

### Rate-Distortion Optimized VQ (RDOGaussian)

RDOGaussian formulates VQ as an entropy-constrained optimization:

\[\min_{\{e_k\}, \{\text{idx}_i\}} \sum_i \|a_i - e_{\text{idx}_i}\|^2 + \lambda H(\{\text{idx}_i\})\]

where \(H\) is the entropy of the index sequence. Minimizing \(H\) pushes indices toward a non-uniform distribution (some codes used frequently, most used rarely), which compresses well with entropy coding. The \(\lambda\) parameter controls the rate-distortion tradeoff.

### Residual VQ (Compact3D, Lee et al.)

Multi-stage codebooks refine the approximation iteratively:
1. Stage 1: encode \(a_i\) with codebook \(C_1\). Residual: \(r_i^{(1)} = a_i - \hat{a}_i^{(1)}\).
2. Stage 2: encode \(r_i^{(1)}\) with codebook \(C_2\). Residual: \(r_i^{(2)} = r_i^{(1)} - \hat{r}_i^{(1)}\).
3. Continue for \(L\) stages.

Each stage reduces the residual. With \(L\) stages and \(K\) codebook entries each, total storage is \(L \log_2 K\) bits per Gaussian, which can be traded against reconstruction quality.

terms.name: Quantized value
terms.desc: The discrete approximation of the original continuous value x. It lives on a regular grid of allowed values spaced Δ apart. Reconstruction error is at most Δ/2.

terms.name: Step size (quantization interval)
terms.desc: The spacing between adjacent quantization levels. Smaller Δ means finer precision and lower error, but requires more bits to index the levels. With B bits you get 2^B levels, so Δ = range / 2^B.

terms.name: Round to nearest level index
terms.desc: Divides x by Δ to get a fractional index, then rounds to the nearest integer. This integer is the codebook index stored in the file — not the value itself.

terms.name: Joint optimisation over codebook and assignments
terms.desc: Simultaneously optimises the K codebook vectors e_k and the per-Gaussian index assignments idx_i. This is harder than standard k-means (which only optimises assignments given fixed centres) but finds better rate-distortion tradeoffs.

terms.name: Reconstruction distortion
terms.desc: Mean squared error between each Gaussian's true attribute vector a_i and its codebook reconstruction. This is the quality loss — minimising it alone gives standard VQ.

terms.name: Rate-distortion tradeoff weight
terms.desc: Controls the balance between compression (small H) and quality (small distortion). Large λ prioritises small file size at the cost of accuracy; small λ prioritises quality. Sweeping λ traces out the rate-distortion curve.

terms.name: Entropy of the index sequence
terms.desc: The theoretical minimum bits needed to store all N Gaussian indices, given their empirical frequency distribution. Minimising H pushes the assignment toward a skewed distribution — a few codes used very often, most used rarely — which compresses well with arithmetic coding.
